Facts.text: Witiges, King of the Ostrogoths, ends his siege of Rome and retreats to Ravenna, leaving the city in the hands of the victorious Byzantine general Belisarius
Chintila becomes the Visigothic king of Hispania upon the death of King Sisenand
Pope Leo IX escapes captivity and returns to Rome
Odo of Lagery is elected as Pope Urban II, succeeding Victor III
Gherardo Caccianemici is elected Pope Lucius II, succeeding Celestine II
Orvieto city in Italy announces it will behead and burn Jewish-Christian couples
University of Vienna is founded
Jews are expelled from Syria
Governor Afonso d'Albuquerque establishes the first overseas Portuguese mint, to issue gold coins in the Indian trading city of Goa
Poet Luís Vaz de Camões publishes the epic poem "Os Lusíadas" in Portugal
A 'Company of Far Lands' established by Dutch merchants in Amsterdam to send ships to the East Indies, beginning of the Dutch East Indies Company
England sends troops to Amiens
Bermuda officially becomes part of the Englisg Colony of Virginia
Dutch settlement on Java changes name to Batavia
Ignatius of Loyola declared a saint
1st naturalization act in American colonies
New Jersey becomes an English colony
1st steam engine in America installed, to pump water from a mine
Jeanne Baptiste Pointe de Sable found settlement now known as Chicago
French Revolution: The National Assembly issues a decree allowing for the sale of church land by French municipalities
Theatre Royal in London's Dury Lane opens after being rebuilt
Austria declares war on France
Romantic ballet "La Sylphide" by Filippo Taglioni premieres at the Opéra de Paris
2nd Republic established in France
1st gold seekers arrive in Nicaragua en route to California
1st US $20 gold piece issued
Desjardins Canal Train Disaster: Canadian Great Western passenger train crashes through rotting timber bridge over Desjardins Canal, near Hamilton, Ontario, killing 59 people
Last French troops leave Mexico
Great Britain annexes Basutoland in Africa (later renamed the Kingdom of Lesotho)
Henry O'Farrell attempts to assassinate Prince Alfred, Duke of Edinburgh, in Sydney, Australia, Duke is shot but survives
US Congress abolishes manufacturer's tax
Great Britain annexes Walvis Bay at Cape colony, Southern Africa.
Andrew Watson makes his Scotland debut as the world's first Black international football player and captain
Mississippi establishes 1st US state college for women
2nd day of the Great blizzard of '88 in north east US (400 die)
Battle at Metema (Gallabad): Ethiopian Emperor Yohannes IV, defeated
Start of South Africa's 1st Test, v England, Port Elizabeth
Pittsburgh issues free season tickets for ladies on Tuesday & Friday
1st movie in Netherlands (Kalverstr 220)
President Steyn of Orange Free state flees from Bloemfontein
Ground is broken for Boston's 1st AL ballpark (Huntington Ave Grounds)
New York Highlanders (Yankees) baseball franchise is approved as a member of the American League
1st main line electric train in UK (Liverpool to Southport)
Heavy storm ravages Dutch west coast
Stanley Cup, Montreal Arena, Westmount, Quebec: Montreal Wanderers beat Winnipeg Maple Leafs, 9-3 for 2-0 sweep of challenge series
The Pan-Macedonian group is formed in Athens to support the Greek Struggle for Macedonia
Alarmed over increasing German naval strength, Parliament passes a new naval appropriations bill
Stanley Cup, Montreal Arena, Westmount, Quebec: Montreal Wanderers beat Berlin Dutchmen (ON), 7-3
Establishment of the first football club in Bulgaria - Botev Plovdiv
Foundation stone of the Australian capital in Canberra laid
French airship mistakenly attacks and sinks British submarine D3 with loss of all hands
In the wake of the February Revolution, Communist Party members Joseph Stalin, Lev Kamenev and Matvei Muranov arrive in Petrograd (St Petersburg) and seize control of the Pravda newspaper
[OS Feb 27] Russian Duma sets up the Provisional Committee; Soviets form Executive Committee
Austrian National Meeting affirms Anschluss (incorporate into Germany)
Denmark begins unilateral disarmament
PopePius XInames J E van Roey archbishop of Malines Belgium
In California, the St. Francis Dam fails, killing over 600 people
Stanislawa Walasiewicz [Stella Walsh] sets world record for the 220-yard dash (0:26.1)
Acting President Constantine Päts commits coup in Tallinn, Estonia
Britain establishes 30 MPH speed limit for towns & villages
Nazi Germany invades Austria (Anschluss)
Finland signs the Moscow Peace Treaty in Moscow, surrendering to Russia and ceding 11% of their pre-WWII territory, ending the "Winter War"
German occupiers confiscate AVRO studios in Netherlands
WWII: British troops vacate the Andaman Islands in Gulf of Bengal
Soviet troops liberate Wjasma
30 Amsterdammers executed by nazi occupiers
Italy's Communist Party (CPI) calls for armed uprising in Italy
NY is 1st to prohibit discrimination by race and creed in employment
USSR returns Transylvania to Romania
Belgian government of Huysmans resigns
-5°F lowest temperature ever recorded in Cleveland, Ohio in month of March (record tied in 1984)
Belgium votes (58%) for return of King Leopold III
PopePius XIIencyclical "On combating atheistic propaganda"
Baseball Commish Happy Chandler loses fight (9-7) to stay in office
Comic strip "Dennis the Menace," 1st appears in the British comic magazine The Beano'
Communist troops driven out of Seoul
Dow Jones Industrial Average closes above 500 for 1st time (500.24)
German DR accepts 22 Russian divisions
British Empire Day is renamed "Commonwealth Day"
Dutch Liberal Party wins 2nd parliamentary elections
US House joins Senate approving Hawaii statehood
Dutch Premier De Quay announces secret talks with Indonesia
S. N. Behrman's play "But for Whom Charlie" premieres in NYC
Union leader Jimmy Hoffa sentenced to 8 years
WKAB TV channel 32 in Montgomery, AL (ABC) begins broadcasting
"Wooly Bully" single released by Sam the Sham and the Pharaohs
Love's 1st album released "Love"
Pioneer Plaza dedicated
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Austria's Reinhold Bachler ski jumps 505 feet
Indian Ocean island nation Mauritius gains independence from Britain (National Day)
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
US lowers voting age from 21 to 18
The Allman Brothers Band record their live album "Live at Fillmore East" on this date and the following day
Thousands of Belfast shipyard workers march demanding the introduction of Internment for members of the Irish Republican Army
Turkish Government of Demirel forced to resign by Army
Rowan & Martin's Laugh-In last airs on NBC-TV
Vietcong conquer Ban me Thuot, South Vietnam
South African troops leave Angola
NY Islanders 3rd scoreless tie, vs Pittsburgh Penguin
Soyuz T-4 carries 2 cosmonauts to Salyut 6 space station
Walter R T Witschey installs world's largest sundial in Richmond, Virginia
PLO chief Yassar Arafat appears on "Nightline"
Don Ritchie runs world record 50 mile (4:51:49)
British National Union of Mine Workers headed by Arthur Scargill supports regional strikes, calls for national action; strikes fail to achieve goals
Susan Butcher wins 1,158 mile Iditarod Trail Sled Dog Race
Ice Pairs World Championship at Cincinnati won by Ekaterina Gordeeva & Sergei Grinkov (USSR)
USSR performs nuclear test at Eastern Kazakh/Semipalitinsk (Soviet Union)
2 cyanide-contaminated Chilean grapes found (Philadelphia)
Madagascar AREMA party wins parliamentary election
LA Raiders announce their return to Oakland
OPEC announces oil production cut to 22.3 Mbbl/d (3,550,000 m3/d)
Mauritius becomes a republic while remaining a member of the Commonwealth of Nations
317 killed by bomb attacks in Bombay
Cleveland radio station WMMS-FM/101.7 is bought by Disney
Entertainment Tonight's 3,000th show
Inkhata leader Mangosuthu Buthelezi begins 2½ week speech to the KwaZulu Legislative Assembly
The Church of England ordains its first 32 female priests at the Bristol Cathedral in Bristol, England
Congress party loses India national election
Ice Dance Championship at Birmingham UK won by Gritshuk & Platov (RUS)
Ice Pairs Championship at Birmingham won by Radka Kovarikova & Rene Novotny
Letitia Vriesde runs South American indoor record 800m (2:00.35)
World Ladies' Figure Skating Champ in Birmingham won by Chen Lu (China)
"The Sound of Music" opens at Martin Beck Theater NYC
Former Warsaw Pact members the Czech Republic, Hungary and Poland join NATO
US crime series "The Shield", created by Shawn Ryan and starring Michael Chiklis, premieres on FX
Elizabeth Smart found after having been missing for 9 months.
Prime Minister of Serbia Zoran Đinđić is assassinated in Belgrade
Music work "I La Galigo" by Richard Wilson debuts in Singapore, based on Bugis creation myth from South Sulawesi (world's most voluminous literary work)
Roh Moo-hyun, President of South Korea, is impeached by its national assembly for the first time in the nation's history.
Tung Chee Hwa, the first Chief Executive of Hong Kong, steps down from his post after his resignation is approved by the Chinese central government.
Streaming service Hulu launches for public access in the United States
A reactor at the Fukushima Daiichi nuclear power plant melts and explodes and releases radioactivity into the atmosphere a day after Japan's earthquake.
100 people are killed in ethnic clashes and cattle raids in South Sudan
45 people, including children, are massacred by the Syrian Army in Homs
China records its highest trade deficit in over a decade
"The Flick", a play by Annie Baker opens off-Broadway at Playwrights Horizons in New York (Pulitzer Prize for Drama 2014)
JOGMEC becomes the first to successfully extract methane hydrate from seabed deposits
The 2013 Papal conclave begins with no new Pope elected on the first day
8 people are killed, 70 injured, & 2 buildings are leveled by a gas explosion in East Harlem, New York
Irene Sankoff and David Hein's musical drama "Come From Away" opens at Schoenfeld Theatre, NYC
Civilian death toll in Eastern Ghouta passes 1,000 in three weeks as Syrian government forces capture the town of Mesraba
Plane crash of Bangladeshi carrier at Kathmandu airport kills at least 49 after plane approaches runway from wrong end
Research shows Chinese cites have decreased pollution by 32 percent on average in just four years
Tens of thousands of farmers from Indian state Maharashtra end protests over loan waivers, prices and land rights after promises from state officials and walking 167km to Mumbai
K-pop and Big Bang singer Seungri is arrested for procuring prostitutes, retires to fight the charges in South Korea
More than 3,000 ISIS fighters have surrendered amid battle for last ISIS stronghold in Baghouz, Syria, according to Syrian Democratic Forces officials
Theresa May's British government suffers a second defeat on a Brexit deal with the EU, 391 votes to 242
NHL pauses the 2019-20 season due to the COVID-19 pandemic
Researchers say world has likely reached 'peak twin' with more twin births than ever before - 1.6 million twins born per year (1 in 42)
Saudi Arabia executes 81 convicted criminals, the country's largest known mass execution in modern times

Cyclone Freddy makes landfall for a second time in central Mozambique, Madagascar and Malawi, killing about 200 people and setting records as the longest-lasting cyclone in the southern hemisphere (formed Feb 6)
Oil company Saudi Aramco posts earnings of $161 billion for 2022 -  highest-ever recorded annual profit by a publicly listed company
A Romanian court rules thatAndrew Tateand his brother can be extradited to the UK only after the Romanian trial for human trafficking
First record ofJohannes Gutenberg's Bible, a letter dated this day by Enea Silvio Piccolomini refers to the Bible printed a year earlier
Former English KingJames IIlands in Ireland
Galileo's body moved to Church of Santa Croce in Florence, Italy
British poet laureate Robert Southey writes in reply to 20 year-oldCharlotte Brontë"Literature cannot be the business of a woman's life, and it ought not to be."
Giuseppe Verdi's opera "Simon Boccanegra" premieres in Venice
Clara SchumannplaysJohannes Brahms's "Variations on a Theme by Haydn" for two pianos, with James Kwast in her last public concert (Frankfurt, Germany)
Vincent d'Indy's opera "Fervaal" premieres in Brussels
Andrew Carnegieestablishes Carnegie Hero Fund
The continuing strikes and disorders that unsettle Italy force out PremierGiovanni Giolitti, though he will return in March, 1906
Girl Scouts begin in the US as the Girl Guides, formed byJuliette Gordon Lowin Savannah
Helen HayesTheater (Little Theatre) opens at 240W 44th St, NYC
A German submarine sinks an unarmed US merchant ship, the 'Algonquin' on the same day that US PresidentWoodrow Wilsongives executive order to arm US merchant ships
Fearing foreign invasion,Vladimir Leninshifts revolutionary Russia's capital from Petrograd (St. Petersburg) to Moscow
George Bernard Shaw's comedy play "Augustus Does His Bit" premieres in NYC
Cairo Conference begins, British meeting to determine Middle Eastern policies,Gertrude BellandT. E. Lawrenceattend
British government ofStanley Baldwinrefuses to ratify Geneva agreement
Franklin D. Rooseveltconducts his first "fireside chat" as US President via radio, speaking directly to 60 million listening Americans, eight days after his inauguration
Communist revolutionaryJosip Broz Titofreed from jail
Paul Hindemith's symphony "Mathis der Maler" premieres in Berlin
PopePius XIIcrowned 260th Supreme Pontiff in a ceremony at the Vatican
US PresidentHarry Trumanintroduces the Truman Doctrine to fight communism
1st performance ofArnold Schoenberg's opera "Moses und Aaron", in a concert setting in Hamburg, Germany
Random House and Houghton-Mifflin co-publish "The Cat in the Hat" byDr. Seuss
the Beatles perform as a trio, at Granada Cinema, in Bedford, England, asJohn Lennonis ill with a cold
6th Grammy Awards: Days of Wine & Roses,Barbra Streisandwins 2
Chicago Black HawksBobby Hull's 51st goal of season, sets record
JockeyJohnny Longdenretires after 40 years (6,032 wins)
Indonesian congress stripsPresident Sukarnoof authority and namesGeneral Suhartoas acting President
11th Grammy Awards: "By the Time I Get to Phoenix" byGlen Campbellwins best record
120 cannabis joints found atGeorge Harrisonand Patti Harrison's home in Esher, England
Hafez al-Assadconsolidates power in Syria by installing himself as President
Ted Bundyvictim Donna Manson disappears from Evergreen State College in Olympia, Washington (body never found)
Chilean dictatorAugusto Pinochetbans the Christian-Democratic Party
Egyptian PresidentAnwar Sadatpledges to regain Arab territory from Israel
American speed skaterEric Heidensets world record in 1000m (1:14.99)
Columbia Records releases "Glass Houses", singer-songwriterBilly Joel's seventh studio album; it features his 1st No.1 single - "It's Still Rock and Roll to Me"
Jury findsJohn Wayne Gacyguilty of murdering 33 in Chicago
Stephen Sondheim's musical revue "Marry Me a Little" premieres in NYC
Larry Birdscores Boston Celtic record 60 points
David Robinsonscores 50 points in a NCAA basketball game
Federal judge dismisses lawsuits sought byOliver North

Computer scientistTim Berners-Leesubmits his first proposal for an "information management system" to his boss at the European Organization for Nuclear Research (CERN) who finds it “vague, but exciting”
Rock for the Rainforest benefit concert held at Carnegie Hall, NYC; performers include:Sting,Elton John,James Taylor,Natalie Cole, and Don Henley
World Men's Figure Skating Champions in Birmingham won byElvis Stojkoof Canada
Animated film "Ice Age" directed by Chris Wedge and Carlos Saldanha, with voices by Denis Leary andJohn Leguizamopremieres
22nd Rock and Roll Hall of Fame inductees: Grandmaster Flash and the Furious Five; R.E.M.; The Ronettes; Patti Smith; andVan Halen
"The Hunger Games" directed by Francis Lawrence and starringJennifer Lawrencepremieres in Los Angeles
British Prime MinisterTheresa Maysays Russia was "highly likely" to have poisoned a Russian spy and his daughter on March 4 with nerve agent
Dozens charged in US college admission scandal by US federal prosecutors, including actresses Lori Loughlin andFelicity Huffman
UK PMBoris Johnsonsays the UK is facing the “worst public health crisis for a generation”, that up to 10,000 people may already be infected with COVID-19
USPresident Trumpbans travel with 26 European countries, though not the UK, due to COVID-19 (UK and Ireland added a day later)
Italian Prime MinisterMario Draghiimposes new COVID restrictions warning of a new wave as cases again exceed 25,000 a day
95th Academy Awards: "Everything Everywhere All at Once" best film, best director Daniel Kwan and Daniel Scheinert,Michelle Yeohsecond woman of color to win best actress,Brendan Fraserbest actor
Half a million Israeli's protest plans byBenjamin Netanyahu's government to overhaul the country’s judicial system for tenth week in a row
Coca-Cola is sold in bottles for the first time in a candy store in Vicksburg, Mississippi
Mahatma Gandhibegins his famous 200-mile (320 km) protest march against the widely hated British salt tax
Prince Charles, Count of Valois (1284-1325), son of King Philip III of France, founder of the House of Valois, born in France (d. 1325)
Edward Duke of Guelders, Count of Zutphen (1361-71), born in the Duchy of Guelders (d. 1371)
Ashikaga Yoshimochi, 4th Japanese shōgun of the Ashikaga shogunate (1394-1423), born in Japan (d. 1428)
Giuliano de' Medici, Italian ruler of Florence (1513-16), born in Florence (d. 1516)
Caspar Othmayr, German composer and Lutheran pastor, born in Amberg, Duchy of Bavaria (d. 1553)
Paul Gerhardt, German Luthern hymnist, born in Gräfenhainichen, Electorate of Saxony (d. 1676)
André Le Nôtre, French landscape architect (gardens of the Palace of Versailles) and principal gardener of King Louis XIV of France, born in Paris, France (d. 1700)[1]
John Aubrey, English antiquary, natural philosopher, and writer, born in Kington St Michael, England (d. 1697)
Anne Hyde, English first wife of James II of England, born in Windsor, England (d. 1671)
Victor Maurice de Broglie, count of Broglie, French Army general and marshal of France, born in Turin, Duchy of Savoy (d. 1727)
George Berkeley, Irish philosopher and bishop of Cloyne who defended idealism, Berkeley, California named after him), born near Kilkenny, Ireland (d. 1753)[1]
Joseph Damer, 1st Earl of Dorchester, English politician in the House of Commons (1741-62), born in England (d. 1798)
Georges Couthon, French politician and lawyer during French Revolution (worked closely with Robespierre in Legislative Assembly), born in Orcet, France (d. 1794)

Carolus Antonius Fodor [Carel Anton Fodor], Dutch pianist, conductor and prominent composer, born in Venlo, Netherlands (d. 1846)
Hester Stanhope, English aristocrat, traveller and archaeologist, born in Chevening, England (d. 1839)
Frederica of Baden, German-born Queen of Sweden (1797-1809), born in Karlsruhe Palace, Grand Duchy of Baden (d. 1826)
Pierre-Jean David d'Angers], French sculptor (Panthéon in Paris), born in Angers, France (d. 1856)
Augustin-Philippe Peellaert, Belgian military officer, painter, composer, and librettist, born in Bruges, Belgium (d. 1876)
William Lyon Mackenzie, Canadian-American newspaper publisher, politician, and failed rebel leader, born in Dundee, Scotland (d. 1861)
Louis-Prosper Gachard, Belgian historian (National Archives of Belgium), born in Paris, France (d. 1885)
Jane Appleton Pierce, American 1st lady (1853-57) and wife of Franklin Pierce, born in Hampton, New Hampshire (d. 1863)
Gerrit van der Linde, "The Schoolmaster", Dutch poet, born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1858)
David Stuart, American politician, Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1868)
John Lorimar Worden, American naval officer for the Union, commandeered USS Monitor during the Battle of Hampton Roads, born in Mount Pleasant, New York (d. 1897)[1]
Luitpold, Prince Regent of Bavaria (regent for nephews King Ludwig II and King Otto), born in Würzburg, Bavaria (d. 1912)
William F. Perry, American Brigadier General in the Confederate Army (Civil War), born in Jackson County, Kentucky (d. 1901)
Gustav Kirchhoff, German physicist (spectroscopy, coined "black body" radiation), born in Königsberg, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1887)
Robert Lowry, American hymn composer, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1899)
John R. Jones, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army) and Virginia educator, born in Rockingham County, Virginia (d. 1901)
William R. Terry, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army) and Virginia Senate, born in Bedford County, Virginia (d. 1897)
William F. Brantley, American lawyer and Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Greene County, Alabama (d. 1870)
Clement Studebaker, American manufacturer of horse-drawn vehicles and later made early automobiles, born in East Berlin, Pennsylvania (d. 1901)
Charles Boycott, Irish estate manager (whose workers refused to work for him amid Irish rent and land issues, origin of the term "boycott"), born in Burgh St. Peter, England (d. 1897)
Simon Newcomb, Canadian-American scientist, mathematician and astronomer (calculated extremely accurate tables for the solar system), born in Wallace, Nova Scotia (d. 1909)
Alexandre Guilmant, French organist (La Trinitié, 1871-1901), composer and pedagogue (Schola Cantorum, 1894-1911), born in Meudon, France (d. 1911)
William Perkin, English chemist (discovered aniline dyes), born in London, England (d. 1907)
John White, American organist and composer based in Germany, born in West Springfield, Massachusetts (d. 1902)
Adolph Ochs, American newspaper publisher, former owner of The New York Times and The Chattanooga Times, born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 1935)
Abraham H. Cannon, American member of the Quorum of the Twelve Apostles of the LDS Church, born in Salt Lake City, Utah Territory  (d. 1896)
Josef Cyril Sychra, Czech composer and choirmaster, born in Ústí nad Orlicí, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1935)
Salvatore Di Giacomo, Italian poet and songwriter during "Golden Age of Neapolitan Song", born in Naples, Kingdom of the Two Sicilies (d. 1934)
Jane Delano, American nurse and educator (Red Cross Nursing Service during WWI), born in Montour Falls, New York (d. 1919)
Vladimir Vernadsky, Russian mineralogist and one of the founders of geochemistry, biogeochemistry, and radiogeology, born in Saint Petersburg, Russian Empire (d. 1945)
Alice Tegnér (née Sandström), Swedish organist, poet, and composer of children's songs, born in Karlshamn, Sweden (d. 1943)
W. H. R. Rivers, English psychiatrist, anthropologist, and neurologist, born in Chatham, Kent (d. 1922)
Edmund Eysler, Austrian composer, mostly of operettas (Bruder Straubinger; The Golden Mistress), born in Hernals, Austria (d. 1949)
Juli Garreta, Spanish-Catalan composer (Sardanes; Les illes Medes), born in Sant Feliu de Guíxols, Spain (d. 1925)

Gemma Galgani, Italian saint and mystic, born near Lucca, Italy (d. 1903)
Joseph Gustav Mraczek, German violinist, conductor, composer (Max und Moritz) and pedagogue, born in Brno, Bohemia (d. 1944)
Henry Drysdale Dakin, English biochemist known for the Dakin-West reaction, born in London (d. 1952)
House Peters, British silent film actor (This Is the Life, Kansas Territory), born in Bristol, England (d. 1967)
Daniel Hoan, American politician (longest Socialist administration in the US as Mayor of Milwaukee 1916-40), born in Waukesha, Wisconsin (d. 1961)
Gunnar Nordström, Finnish theoretical physicist (gravitation), born in Helsinki, Finland (d. 1923)
Judge Jackson, American sacred harp composer, songwriter, and educator (The Colored Sacred Harp), born in Montgomery County, Alabama (d. 1958)
Zoltán Meskó, leading Hungarian Nazi, born in Baja, Austria-Hungary (d. 1959)
Raphaël Verwilghen, Belgian architect, and urban planner, born in Roeselare, West Flanders, Belgium (d. 1963)

Evert Axel Taube, Swedish ballad singer and composer, born in Gothenburg, Sweden (d. 1976)
Vaslav Nijinsky, Ukrainian legendary ballet dancer (Petroesjka - Ballets Russes) and choreographer, born in Kyiv, Russian Empire (d. 1950) [OS=Feb 28]
William Dudley Pelley, American fascist activist and leader of the Silver Legion in America, born in Lynn, Massachusetts (d. 1965)
George W. Mason, American industrialist and CEO of the Kelvinator Corporation (1928-37), born in Valley City, North Dakota (d. 1954)
Michael Polanyi, Hungarian-English chemist, economist and philosopher (claimed all knowledge relied on personal judgments), born in Budapest, Austria-Hungary (d. 1976)
William C. Lee, American U.S. Army general and 'Father of the US Airboure', born in Dunn, North Carolina (d. 1948)
Jesse Fuller, American one-man blues band ("San Francisco Bay Blues"), born in Jonesboro, Georgia (d. 1979)
Zoltán Vásárhelyi, Hungarian violinist, teacher, and choral conductor, born in Kecskemét, Hungary (d. 1977)
Ken James, New Zealand cricket wicket-keeper (11 Tests, 16 dismissals; Wellington, Northamptonshire CCC), born in Wellington, New Zealand (d. 1976)
Arthur Hewlett, British actor (The Pied Piper), born in Southampton, England (d. 1997)
E. Dorrit Hoffleit, American astronomer who studied visible stars (The Bright Star Catalogue), born in Florence, Alabama (d. 2007)
David Marshall, Singaporean politician (Chief Minister of Singapore 1955-56, pushed self-governance for Singapore), born in Singapore (d. 1995)
Max Wall [Lorimer], British music hall, screen, and stage comic and actor (Professor Wallofski), born in Stockwell, England (d. 1990)
Rita Angus [Henrietta Catherine Angus], New Zealand painter (Cass, 1936), born in Hastings, New Zealand (d. 1970)
Masayoshi Ohira, Prime Minister of Japan (1978-80), born in Kan'onji, Japan (d. 1980)
Roger L. Stevens, American real estate magnate, Broadway theater producer (West Side Story) and fundraiser (John F. Kennedy Center), born in Detroit, Michigan (d. 1998)[1]
Tony "Two-Ton" Galento, American heavyweight boxer and actor (On the Waterfront), born in Orange, New Jersey (d. 1979)
Gustavo Diaz Ordaz, Mexican politician (President of Mexico 1964-70), born in San Andrés Chalchicomula, Mexico (d. 1979)
Irving Layton, Romanian-Canadian poet (A Red Carpet for the Sun), born in Târgu Neamţ, Romania (d. 2006)
Kylie Tennant, Australian novelist (Battlers, Lost Haven), born in Manly, Australia (d. 1988)

Paul Weston [Wetstein], American pianist, arranger, orchestra leader (Tommy Dorsey Orchestra; The Jim Nabors Show), pop and classical composer ("Day By Day"; "Crescent City Suite"), born in Springfield, Massachusetts (d. 1996)
Agathe von Trapp, Austrian-born American singer (oldest von Trapp child), born in Pola, Austro-Hungarian Empire (d. 2010)
Harold Butler, English cricket fast bowler (2 Tests, 12 wickets; Nottinghamshire CCC), born in Clifton, Nottinghamshire, England (d. 1991)
Jan Kapr, Czech composer, born in Prague, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1988)
Julia Lennon, English mother of musician John Lennon (The Beatles), born in Liverpool, England (d. 1958)
Alberto Burri, Italian abstract painter, born in Città di Castello, Italy (d. 1995)
Red Byron, American auto racer (NASCAR Strictly Stock Series C'ship 1949), born in Plasterco, Virginia (d. 1960)
Googie Withers, English actress and dancer (The Lady Vanishes), born in Karachi, British India (d. 2011)
Leonard Chess [Lejzor Czyż], Polish-American record company executive (Chess Records), and blues and R&B record producer, born in Motal, Poland (d. 1969)
Elaine de Kooning, American artist, born in Brooklyn, New York City (d. 1989)
Frank Overton, American actor (Fail Safe; 12 O'Clock High (TV series, not film)), born in Babylon, New York (d. 1967)
(Giovanni) "Gianni" Agnelli, Italian industrialist, executive (President of Fiat Automobiles, 1966-96), and Italian Senator-for-life (1991-2003), born in Turin, Italy (d. 2003)
Gordon MacRae, American singer and actor (Oklahoma!; Carousel), born in East Orange, New Jersey (d. 1986)
Ralph Shapey, American composer (Fantasy; Rituals), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2002)
Ülo Jõgi, Estonian freedom fighter and war historian, born in Tallinn, Estonia (d. 2007)

Thomas Hugh Eastwood, British composer, born in Hawley, Blackwater, England (d. 1999)

Hjalmar Andersen, Norwegian speed skater (Olympic gold 1,500m, 5,000m, 10,000m 1952; World C'ship gold all-round 1950, 51, 52), born in Rødøy, Norway (d. 2013)
Mae Young, American professional wrestler and one of the pioneers in women's wrestling (National Wrestling Alliance, WWF), born in Sand Springs, Oklahoma (d. 2014)
Norbert Brainin, Austrian-British violinist (Amadeus Quartet, 1947-87), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 2005)
Walter "Wally" Schirra Jr, American US Navy Captain and NASA astronaut (Mercury 8, Gemini 6, Apollo 7), born in Hackensack, New Jersey (d. 2007)
G. William Whitehurst, American politician (Rep-R-VA 1969-87), born in Norfolk, Virginia
Georges Delerue, French film composer (Sword of Gideon; A Little Romance), born in Roubaix, France (d. 1992)
Harry Harrison, American -Irish, sci-fi author (Deathworld trilogy), born in Stamford, Connecticut (d. 2012)
